QF.id: XIII Introduction to the Underlying Markets_75

question.text: What is the significance of the 'repo rate' set by RBI, and how does it connect all three underlying markets?

answer.text: The repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends to banks — it influences short-term money market rates, bond yields (through the yield curve), equity valuations (through discount rates), and currency rates (through interest rate differentials)
The repo rate only affects the banking sector and has no impact on equity or currency markets
The repo rate determines the exact price of all government bonds
The repo rate is set by SEBI and applies only to derivative margin calculations

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The repo rate is the central policy rate: (1) It anchors short-term money market rates, (2) It influences the yield curve and bond prices, (3) It affects equity valuations through the discount rate (higher repo = higher discount = lower equity values), (4) It impacts currency markets through interest rate differentials and IRP. **The Trap:** The repo rate affects ALL financial markets, not just banking. It doesn't directly set bond prices but influences them through market expectations. It's set by RBI, not SEBI. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV covers repo rate's impact on bond markets. Series VIII covers its impact on equity derivative pricing. Series I covers interest rate differentials for currency pricing. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** RBI policy decisions are among the most impactful events for derivative markets — all three markets (bonds, equities, currencies) react simultaneously to repo rate changes.
